title: Introduction to Vectors
description: What is a vector? Creating vectors with c(), :, seq(), rep().

slides.title: What is a Vector? 🧩
slides.content:
# Vectors: The Building Blocks of R

A **vector** is a sequence of data elements of the same basic type. In R, even a single number is a vector of length 1. Vectors are the most fundamental data structure.

Key properties:
* All elements must have the same type (numeric, character, logical, etc.).
* If you mix types, R will coerce them to the most flexible type (character > numeric > logical).
* Vectors are one-dimensional.

Example:
```r
c(1, 2, 3)        # numeric vector
c("a", "b", "c")  # character vector
c(TRUE, FALSE)    # logical vector
```

slides.title: Creating Vectors with c()
slides.content: The `c()` function (combine) is the most common way to create vectors:
slides.codeSnippet:
num_vec <- c(10, 20, 30)
char_vec <- c('apple', 'banana', 'cherry')
log_vec <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)

print(num_vec)
print(char_vec)
print(log_vec)

# Mixing types: coercion to character
mixed <- c(1, 'two', TRUE)
print(mixed)

quizData.question: What is the type of the vector created by `c(1, TRUE, "three")`?
quizData.explanation: When mixing types, R coerces to the most flexible type: character > numeric > logical. So all become character.

slides.title: Other Creation Methods: Colon Operator ➡️
slides.content:
The colon operator `:` creates integer sequences:

```r
1:5        # 1 2 3 4 5
5:1        # 5 4 3 2 1
2.5:6.5    # 2.5 3.5 4.5 5.5 6.5 (step = 1)
```

slides.title: seq() Function: Flexible Sequences 🔢
slides.content:
`seq()` gives you more control:

```r
seq(from = 1, to = 10, by = 2)    # 1 3 5 7 9
seq(from = 0, to = 1, length.out = 5)  # 0.00 0.25 0.50 0.75 1.00
seq(along.with = c(1,2,3))        # 1 2 3 (indexes)
```

slides.title: seq in Action
slides.codeSnippet:
seq(1, 10, by = 2)
seq(0, 1, length.out = 6)
seq_len(5)  # 1:5
seq_along(c('a','b','c'))  # 1 2 3

slides.title: rep(): Replicate Values 🔁
slides.content:
`rep()` repeats values:

```r
rep(1:3, times = 2)        # 1 2 3 1 2 3
rep(1:3, each = 2)         # 1 1 2 2 3 3
rep(1:3, length.out = 5)   # 1 2 3 1 2
rep(c('a','b'), times = c(2,3))  # 'a' 'a' 'b' 'b' 'b'
```

slides.title: rep Examples
slides.codeSnippet:
rep(4, 5)
rep(1:3, each = 3)
rep(c('x','y'), times = c(3,2))

quizData.question: What is the output of `rep(1:2, each = 3, times = 2)`?
quizData.explanation: First each = 3 creates 1 1 1 2 2 2, then times = 2 repeats that whole vector twice.

slides.title: Create Your Own Vectors
slides.content: Create a vector that repeats the sequence 5,10,15 three times, and another vector that goes from 20 down to 10 in steps of 0.5.
slides.codeSnippet:
# Your code here
vec1 <- rep(c(5,10,15), times = 3)
vec2 <- seq(20, 10, by = -0.5)
print(vec1)
print(vec2)
